coredata.dc:title: Graphene/quantum dot bionanoconjugates as signal amplifiers in stripping voltammetric detection of EpCAM biomarkers
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2012 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Shiddiky, Muhammad J.A.
Rauf, Sakandar
Kithva, Prakash H.
Trau, Matt
coredata.dc:description: A sensitive electrochemical immunosensor for the detection of epithelial cell adhesion molecule (EpCAM) antigen, a common marker for tumors of epithelial origin, employing bionanoconjugates as signal-transduction labels has been developed. The bionanoconjugates were fabricated by carboxylation of the two-dimensional graphene oxide nanosheets (GRs) and immobilizing streptavidin and amine-functionalized CdSe quantum dots (QDs) on carboxylated GRs via carbodiimide coupling chemistry, followed by the immunoreaction with the biotinylated secondary antibodies. Since carboxylated GRs have a higher density of active sites, it allows a large number of CdSe QDs to be immobilized onto the surface of the bionanoconjugates, and hence, enhance the sensitivity of the immunosensor. The method enabled detection limits of 100fg/mL and 1pg/mL (based on the S/N=3) in PBS buffer and serum samples, respectively, using anodic stripping voltammetric readout. The immunosensor showed a good selectivity, reproducibility, and long-storage stability, and may become a promising technique for the early detection of tumor biomarker in clinical/biological samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemical immunosensor
Signal amplification
Graphene oxide nanosheet
Quantum dots
Tumor biomarkers

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Confocal spectroscopy and transmission electron microscopy
Square-wave anodic stripping voltammetric detection of EpCAM

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Breast Cancer Foundation Collaborative Research

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the UQ Postdoctoral Fellowship ( RM 2010001241 ), UQ ECR Grant ( RM 2010002245 ), and National Breast Cancer Foundation Collaborative Research Grant . Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the fabrication of immunosensor and anodic stripping voltammetric detection of tumor biomarker.

Nyquist plot for the electrochemical impedance measurements of (a) bare ITO, (b) ITO/PPA, (c) ITO/PPA/Ab1, (d) ITO/PPA/Ab1/An, and (e) ITO/PPA/Ab1/An/Ab2-GR-QDs in a solution of 1M KCl containing 10mM [Fe(CN)6]3− and 10mM [Fe(CN)6]4−.
Confocal microscopy images of (a) ITO/PPA/Ab1/An/Ab2-GR-QDs and (b) ITO/PPA/Ab1/Ab2-GR-QDs layers onto the ITO substrates. The concentration of EpCAM antigen is 10ng/mL and the scale bar is 50μm.

(a) Anodic stripping voltammograms recorded for the immunosensors in PBS at EpCAM antigen concentration at 10ng/mL employing Ab2-GR-QDs (i) and Ab2-QDs (ii) bionanoconjugates. Inset, voltammograms obtained using Ab2-GR-QDs bionanoconjugates with no spiked EpCAM antigen (0ng/mL) (iii) and 100fg/mL antigen in PBS (iv). (b) Anodic stripping voltammograms of ITO/PPA/Ab1/An/Ab2-GR-QDs immunosensor at EpCAM antigen concentration of (a) 100 fg/mL, (b) 750 fg/mL, (c) 10pg/mL, (d) 50pg/mL, (e) 250pg/mL, (f) 1ng/mL, (g) 10ng/mL, (h) 500ng/mL, and (i) 7.5μg/mL. (c) The immunoassay responses over the concentration range tested. Inset shows the immunoassay responses at lowest four concentrations to illustrate the LOD. (d) Detection of EpCAM in spiked serum samples. Inset, comparison of the blank responses obtained in PBS and serum. Each data point in (c) and (d) represent the average of the three measurements (e.g., n =3).

head.ce:title: Graphene/quantum dot bionanoconjugates as signal amplifiers in stripping voltammetric detection of EpCAM biomarkers

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Australian Institute for Bioengineering & Nanotechnology (AIBN), Centre for Biomarker Research and Development, The University of Queensland, QLD 4072, Australia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Australian Institute for Bioengineering & Nanotechnology (AIBN), School of Chemistry & Molecular Biosciences, Centre for Biomarker Research and Development, The University of Queensland, QLD 4072, Australia

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Australian Institute for Bioengineering & Nanotechnology (AIBN), Centre for Biomarker Research and Development, The University of Queensland, QLD 4072, Australia. Tel.: +61 7 33464169; fax: +61 7 33463973.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: ▸ A simple signal amplification route has been developed for sensitive electrochemical detection of EpCAM antigens. ▸ A ‘bionanoconjugate’ comprised of graphene oxide nanosheets decorated with CdSe QDs and specific antibodies used as an electrochemical signal amplifier. ▸ High density of active sites on the carboxylated graphene oxide nanosheets allows higher number of immobilized CdSe QDs and antibodies onto it. ▸ The use of bionanoconjugate allows a single EpCAM antibody-antigen binding event gives rise to a “burst” of cadmium ions.

A sensitive electrochemical immunosensor for the detection of epithelial cell adhesion molecule (EpCAM) antigen, a common marker for tumors of epithelial origin, employing bionanoconjugates as signal-transduction labels has been developed. The bionanoconjugates were fabricated by carboxylation of the two-dimensional graphene oxide nanosheets (GRs) and immobilizing streptavidin and amine-functionalized CdSe quantum dots (QDs) on carboxylated GRs via carbodiimide coupling chemistry, followed by the immunoreaction with the biotinylated secondary antibodies. Since carboxylated GRs have a higher density of active sites, it allows a large number of CdSe QDs to be immobilized onto the surface of the bionanoconjugates, and hence, enhance the sensitivity of the immunosensor. The method enabled detection limits of 100fg/mL and 1pg/mL (based on the S/N=3) in PBS buffer and serum samples, respectively, using anodic stripping voltammetric readout. The immunosensor showed a good selectivity, reproducibility, and long-storage stability, and may become a promising technique for the early detection of tumor biomarker in clinical/biological samples.

ce:section.ce:para: The increasing demand in “early detection” has promoted the development of efficient tools for the sensitive assays of tumor biomarkers. These assays commonly rely on certain affinity ligands, such as antibodies or aptamers that specifically interact with target proteins and thus mediate a target-responsive signal transduction cascade (Rusling et al., 2010; Giljohana and Mirkin, 2009; Katz and Willner, 2004). Over the past few decades, a great effort has been made towards sensitivity enhancement through the signal amplification and/or employment of new detection technologies that provided a readout in the form of fluorescence, chemiluminescence, surface Plasmon resonance, colorimetric or electrochemical signals (Wu et al., 2007; Sardesai et al., 2009; Li et al., 2010a,b; Rissin et al., 2010; Privett et al., 2008; Shiddiky et al., 2007, 2008, 2010; Wang et al., 2009a,b). Among these detection formats, the electrochemical assays that utilize nanoparticle-based signal amplification platforms offer elegant ways of interfacing biorecognition and transduction events and represent a substantial driver to achieve highly sensitive detection of tumor biomarkers (Wang, 2006; Yu et al. 2006; Tang et al., 2011; Wilson and Nie, 2006; Zhao et al., 2011). Herein we report the proof-of-concept of a signal amplification strategy employing graphene oxide nanosheets (GRs)/quantum dots (QDs)-based bionanoconjugates as electroactive labels in anodic stripping voltammetric detection of a tumor biomarker.

Typically, signal amplification in electrochemical immunoassays depends on the type of electroactive labels used, which are often proteins- and enzyme-functionalized nanoparticles (Gill et al., 2008; Song et al., 2010). They can be used either as labels for direct voltammetric detection or as carriers of electroactive species (e.g., ferrocene or redox enzymes) to amplify the voltammetric responses. To date, much effort has been focused on the development of nanoparticle-based immunoassays for the amplified detection of proteins (Rusling et al., 2010; Cui et al., 2007; Chen et al., 2009; Akanda et al., 2011; Mason et al., 2006). Bar-coded nanoparticles, antibody-functionalized gold nanoparticles, nanowire transistors, enzyme-decorated carbon nanotube, and enzyme-labeled beads used with electrochemical readout have been shown the great potential for development of sensitive immunoassays. However, it still remains a major challenge to the development of immunoassays for the early detection of tumor biomarkers with improved simplicity, selectivity and sensitivity. One way to achieve this goal is to use bionanoconjugates derived from multiple nanomaterials (e.g., GRs, QDs, etc.) as signal-transduction labels. Via this process (Fig. 1 ), a single molecule capture event (e.g., antibody or target) can associate several functionalized-nanomaterials, such as CdSe QDs onto the sensing layer. Dissolution of the QDs gives rise to a “burst” of metal (e.g., Cd2+) ions which can easily be detected via anodic stripping voltammetry. Such a methodology represents a simple and accurate way to significantly/physically amplify the detection signal. Furthermore, we believe that such a simple signal amplification event may be of significant use in future point-of-care applications in molecular diagnostics.

Quantum dots are currently under active investigation for use as electrochemical and fluorescent signal-transduction labels due to their unique chemical and physical properties (Rauf et al., 2009; Wang et al., 2003). In particular, electrochemical activities combined with the biocompatibility and bioconjugation properties of QDs are very attractive to achieve antibody-QDs conjugates as electroactive labels for the voltammetric detection of proteins (Cui et al., 2007). On the other hand, two-dimensional (2D) structure of GRs (2D-GRs) has recently been attracted great attention due to its high surface area, low cost, and high conductivity (Loh et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2010; Guo et al., 2010a,b; Paivi et al., 2010). The large surface area of 2D-GR nanosheet with abundance of active surface functional groups could be easily accessible for the binding of biomolecules, such as drugs (Liu et al., 2008) enzymes or proteins (Xu et al., 2011; Du et al., 2011; Liu et al., 2010), and functionalized-QDs (Guo and Dong, 2011; Li et al., 2011; Wang et al., 2011a,b). By exploiting these properties of GR nanosheets, a new class of bionanoconjugate can easily be fabricated using GR nanosheets, CdSe QDs, and antibodies, where GRs will act as carrier for large amount of QDs and antibodies. These bionanoconjugates will significantly amplify the electrochemical signal because they contain greater amount of QDs per immunological reaction event compare to that afforded by the QD-antibody conjugate. Recently, Yang et al. (2011) reported the use of graphene nanosheets synthesized from graphene oxide using a thermal exfoliation method, on which CdS QDs have been precipitated from a solution of CdCl2 and Na2S in presence of mercaptoundecanoic acid. Antibodies were attached via EDC/NHS coupling on the acid group on the CdS QDs. The approach reported here differs in that a chemical method used to convert oxygen containing groups of graphene oxide (e.g., ester, hydroxyl, and epoxide) into the carboxylic acid groups, which allows greater amount of active sites for the covalent attachment of CdSe QDs and biomolecules. This creates a bionanoconjugate with much better sensitivity than that reported in Yang et al.

EpCAM antigen is a cell surface proteins, and is overexpressed by carcinomas of lung, colorectal, breast, prostate, head, neck, and hepatic origin, and is absent from haematologic cells (Went et al., 2004; Balzar et al., 1999). Recently, antibodies against EpCAM antigens have widely been used to capture and quantify the tumour cells in patients with cancer of epithelial origin (Nagrath et al., 2007). Thus, the development of sensitive immunoassays for EpCAM antigen would be transformative in the treatment of cancers. Herein we report on an electrochemical immunoassay for the detection of EpCAM antigen using antibody-GR-QDs bionanoconjugates as signal-transduction labels. The 2D-GRs were first carboxylated, and streptavidin and amine-functionalized QDs were then attached. Immunoreactions of EpCAM antigens (An) with the surface bound “capture” antibodies (Ab1) was then followed by the immobilization of biotinalyed secondary antibodies (Ab2)-functionalized-bionanoconjugates. After the labeling step, an acid treatment was used to dissolve the surface bound QDs, which was then detected by square-wave anodic stripping voltammetry (SWASV).

Human EpCAM/TROP-1 monoclonal antibody (produced in mouse), human EpCAM/TROP-1 biotinylated polyclonal antibody (produced in goat), recombinant human EpCAM and prostate specific antigens (PSA) were purchased from R & D Systems (USA). N-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 2-(N-morpholino) ethanesulfonic acid (MES) hydrate, sodium choloroacetate, and 3-phosphonopropinic acid (PPA) were also purchased from Sigma–Aldrich (USA). Single layer graphene oxide aqueous suspension (0.1–0.5mg/mL) was obtained from Nanocs, Inc. (Boston, MA). Qdot® 525 ITK™ amino (polyethylene glycol) and Qdot® 525 ITK™ carboxylated quantum dots, streptavidin, and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were purchased from Invitrogen (Australia). All other reagents were obtained from Sigma–Aldrich (USA) and used without further purification. The phosphate buffer saline (PBS) solution consisted of 0.01M phosphate, 0.138M NaCl, and 0.0027M KCl (pH 7.4). The MES buffer comprised of 100mM MES hydrate (pH 5.2).

Firstly, carboxyl-functionalization of the GR nanosheets were carried out following the modified version of the protocol described by Du et al. (2011) (Fig. S1). Briefly, 25mg NaOH and 25mg ClCH2COONa were dissolved in 2mL GR suspension, the solution was sonicated for 2.5h, followed by the neutralization with 0.1M HCl. The resulted carboxylated GR suspension was purified with repetitive rinsing and centrifugation (12,000rpm for 7min) until the product was homogenously dispersed in water, followed by dialysis against water for 72h. The mixture was centrifuged at 12,000rpm for 7min, and the supernatant was discarded. This was then mixed with 1.0mL of 400mM EDC and 100mM NHS in 100mM MES (pH 5.2) buffer and stirred at room temperature for 15min. The resulting mixture was centrifuged at 12,000rpm for 7min, and the supernatant was discarded. The buffer wash was repeated three times to remove excess EDC and NHS. The mixture was dispersed in 1.0mL of PBS buffer (pH 7.4). Next, 50μL streptavidin at 10μg/mL and 50μL of amino-QDs solution at 8μM were added to the solution and stirred overnight at 4°C. The reaction mixture was then centrifuged at 12,000rpm for 7min, and supernatant was removed. The PBS buffer was added to the solid conjugate remaining in the vial, mixed well, and centrifuged again at 12,000rpm for 7min, and the supernatant was discarded. This step served to remove any free streptavidin and QDs, and was repeated three times. The GR-QDs-streptavidin conjugates were redispersed in 1.0mL of PBS containing 50μg/mL biotinalyed EpCAM antibody and incubated overnight at 4°C with stirring (hereafter, referred as Ab2-GR-QDs). The reaction mixture was then centrifuged at 12,000rpm for 7min, and the supernatant was discarded. The buffer wash was repeated four times to remove any free antibody. 1.0mL PBS buffer containing 2% BSA and 0.05% Tween-20 was then added to the vial, mixed well, and stored at 4°C.

A schematic preparation of the immunosensing layer is shown in Fig. S2. Unless otherwise stated, the surface functionalization, antibody attachment, and immunoreaction procedures were as follows: diced ITO coated glass substrates (0.3cm×2.0cm each) (Nanocs, Inc., Boston, MA) were successively cleaned with ethanol, acetone, 2-propanol, and water and then dried at 60°C. The cleaned substrates were pretreated in a mixture containing H2O, H2O2 (30%), NH4OH (30%) in a ratio of 5:1:1 at 70°C for 2h to ensure the presence of active hydroxyl groups on the surface (Akanda et al., 2011). The substrates were then washed three times with water and dried under nitrogen flow. The pretreated substrates were immersed in an aqueous solution containing 0.1mM PPA for 36h at room temperature to allow the formation of a carboxylate monolayer. The substrates were then washed with distilled water. The carboxylic groups of the monolayer were activated by immersing the substrates in a mixture of 400mM EDC and 100mM NHS in pH 5.2 MES buffer for 2h. This was immediately followed by 2h incubation at 37°C with 50μg/mL primary anti-EPCAM antibody (Ab1) in PBS buffer containing 0.05% Tween-20. To prevent nonspecific protein binding, the ITO/Ab1 substrates were then incubated for 1h at 37°C in PBS buffer containing 3% BSA+0.05% Tween-20. For the attachment of target EpCAM, the substrates were then incubated at 37°C for 1h in PBS buffer or 98% human serum containing designated concentration of EpCAM antigen. Spiked serum samples were made by adding 10μL EpCAM solution (in PBS) in to 500μL of serum. Next, these substrates were incubated in as-prepared Ab2-GR-QDs bionanoconjugate for 2h at 37°C to achieve the ITO/PPA/Ab1/An/Ab2-GR-QDs immunosensor (Fig. 1). Each of the steps involved in the immobilization of the antibody, EpCAM, and bionanoconjugate were followed by washing three times with PBS buffer containing 0.05% Tween-20 buffer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Confocal spectroscopy and transmission electron microscopy
ce:section.ce:para: Confocal microscopy images for the ITO/PPA/Ab1/An/Ab2-GR-QDs immunosensors were taken using Zeiss LSM 710 confocal microscope. The fluorescence signal of QDs 525 was acquired using the LSM software defined excitation (405nm) and emission wavelengths for QDs 525. The objective lens LD Plan-Neofluar ×20 with N.A. 0.4 was used to acquire fluorescence images of quantum dots. A JEOL 1010 Transmission electron microscope (TEM) operating at 100kV was used to observe the morphology of bionanoconjugates. The samples were prepared by dropping 10μl of the Ab2-GR-QDs on the copper grid and allowed to dry.

All electrochemical experiments were conducted at 21±1°C in a standard three-electrode electrochemical cell arrangement using an electrochemical analyzer CHI 650D (CH Instruments, Austin, TX). In electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) experiments, the cell consisted of a ITO-modified substrate as a working electrode, a Pt wire counter electrode, and a Ag/AgCl (3M NaCl) reference electrode. The EIS spectra were recorded in a 10mM K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] (1:1) in 1.0M KCl electrolyte solution using an alternating current voltage of 5mV, with the frequency range of 0.1–10kHz.

To perform SWASV measurements, the surface bound CdSe QDs were dissolved by flushing the ITO/PPA/Ab1/An/Ab2-GR-QDs layer with 250μL of 0.1M HCl solution for 2h, and the resulting solution was then transferred in an electrochemical cell containing 1.75mL of 100mM MES (pH 5.2) buffer. The three-electrode arrangement used for the SWASV experiments consisted of a glassy carbon working electrode, a Pt wire counter electrode, and a Ag/AgCl (3M NaCl) reference electrode. The SWASV experiments involved a 60s pretreatment at +600mV, a 600s accumulation at −1200mV (with stirring), 15s rest period (without stirring) and square-wave voltammetric scan from −1200 to −250mV using a step potential of 4mV, amplitude 25mV, and frequency 25Hz.

To achieve ultrasensitive detection of tumor biomarkers, we synthesized bionanoconjugates bearing 2D-GR nanosheets, QDs, and biotinalyted antibodies (see Fig. S1). According to the TEM and Scanning Electron Microscopy images provided by the manufacturer, the smooth surface of GR retained with the flake size of about 500nm and the thickness of single atomic layers over 80% (carbon and oxygen contents are around 80% and 20%, respectively). It is also important to note that there are many ester, hydroxyl, and epoxide groups on 2D-GRs (Du et al., 2011). Therefore, the conversion of these groups into carboxylic acid groups is necessary to enhance active sites of GRs for the attachment of amine containing molecules via carbidiimide coupling chemistries. In our approach, we used EDC/NHS coupling protocol to immobilize amine-functionalized QDs and streptavidin on the carboxylated-GR nanosheet by the formation of amide bond between carboxylic groups of GR and amines groups of QDs and streptavidin. Immobilization of biotinalyted antibodies onto the surface of the streptavidin-GR-QDs was attained via biotin-streptavidin binding. The TEM images shown in Fig. S3 confirm the homogeneous and dense coverage of QDs on graphene surface. No aggregation of GRs was found. Similar morphological characteristics were previously reported on the GR/QDs nanoconjugates (Li et al., 2011; Wang et al., 2011a,b).

The antibody immobilization and immunoreactions on the ITO-coated glass substrates were first characterized by Faradaic EIS. The self-assembled monolayer of PPA on to ITO substrates and sequential immobilization of antibodies and antigens act as a barrier for the interfacial charge transfer reaction of the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− process, resulting in an increased electron-transfer resistance (R et). Semicircle diameter of an EIS spectrum obtained at higher frequencies corresponds to R et and the linear part at lower frequencies corresponds to the diffusion process (Bard and Faulkner, 2001). Fig. 2 shows the Nyquist plots of EIS spectra obtained at the (a) bare ITO, (b) ITO/PPA, (c) ITO/PPA/Ab1, (d) ITO/PPA/Ab1/An, and (e) ITO/PPA/Ab1/An/Ab2-GR-QDs substrates. The bare ITO electrode revealed a very small semicircle domain (lower R et), indicating a faster electron transfer (a). A slightly larger semicircle, and hence, increased R et at the ITO/PPA-modified electrode indicates the formation of the PPA self assembled monolayer on ITO substrate (b), which hinders the charge transfer reaction the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− process. With the immobilization of primary antibody (c) and 10ng/mL EpCAM antigen (d), the ITO/PPA substrates showed the largest increase in R et, suggesting that the antibody and antigen layers formed additional barriers and further prevented the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− process to the electrode surface. The final attachment of the Ab2-GR-QDs bionanoconjugates onto the ITO/PPA/Ab1/An layer decreased the charge transfer resistance. This might be due to the presence of highly conducting GR nanosheets on the immunosensor surface, which could lower the impedance of the film and facilitate the charge transfer reaction of the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− process. All of these results clearly testified to the formation of the PPA monolayer and stepwise immobilization of the antibodies and antigens on the ITO/PPA-modified substrates.

The application of Ab2-GR-QDs bionanoconjugates in the sandwich immunoassay was further studied by confocal spectroscopy. It is well-known that the blocking with a large amount of BSA (e.g., >3%) and use of minimal incubation time (e.g., 1h) for protein attachment can be beneficial for minimizing the nonspecific protein adsorption on the sensing layer (Du et al., 2010, 2011). Thus, we blocked the ITO/PPA/Ab1 substrates with 3% BSA in PBS at 37°C for 1h. The BSA-blocked substrates were incubated in EpCAM solution in PBS at 37°C for 1h followed by incubation in Ab2-GR-QDs bionanoconjugates in PBS at 37°C for 2h. Fig. 3a shows the confocal image of the ITO/PPA/Ab1/An/Ab2-GR-QDs immunosensor. A strong green fluorescence was observed at the Ab2-GR-QDs-modified areas, indicating that the Ab2-GR-QDs were successfully attached to the immunosensor surface via immunoreactions between EpCAM and bionanoconjugate-attached antibodies. Control image (Fig. 3b) obtained for the immunosensor prepared through the same experimental procedure without exposure to target EpCAM. Clearly, the image found at the control immunosensor had a very low number of green fluorescence spots, indicating the presence of low level of nonspecific adsorption at the ITO/PPA/Ab1 immunosensing layer. It should be noted that an obvious readout method for the surface-bound QDs is to use their fluorescent properties, many of such assays have also been reported in the past (Katz and Willner, 2004). In this study, we have focused on an electrochemical read-out primarily as we can see such systems as potentially being more readily implementable in point-of-care applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Square-wave anodic stripping voltammetric detection of EpCAM
ce:section.ce:para: The immunosensor described in this study was based on the Ab2-GR-QDs as signal-transduction labels in electrochemical stripping voltammetric detection. Stripping voltammetry is a powerful technique for the analysis of trace level of metal ions due to the preconcentration step involved in the method, during which the target metal ions (e.g., Cd2+) are accumulated onto the working electrode (Bard and Faulkner, 2001). The surface bound CdSe QDs were dissolved in 0.1M HCl. During the SWASV scan, the dissolved Cd2+ ions are accumulated onto the glassy carbon electrode. The peak current associated with the oxidation of accumulated cadmium is directly proportional to the amount of target EpCAM antigen captured in the immunosensing layer. Fig. 4a(i) and (iv) (also see Fig. 4a inset) show stripping voltammograms obtained for 10ng/mL and 100fg/mL EpCAM antigen, respectively. In both cases, well-defined stripping peaks with 33.47 and 0.79μA currents at around −850mV were observed, corresponds to the oxidation of cadmium, suggesting the successful binding of EpCAM antigen in the sensing layer, and the use of bionanoconjugates as an electrochemical signal-transduction label. In a control experiment, the BSA-blocked ITO/PPA/Ab1 substrate was incubated directly to Ab2-GR-QDs bionanoconjugates solution without exposure to target EpCAM (hereafter, refer to as ITO/PPA/Ab1/Ab2-GR-QDs). A peak current of 0.25μA at the similar potential region was observed (Fig. 4a(iii) and inset in Fig. 4a). This result, in accordance with Fig. 3b, again indicates nonspecific adsorption of Ab2-GR-QDs bionanoconjugates onto the sensing layer. Most importantly, this small response is about 3-times lower than that obtained at the sensing layer in presence of 100fg/mL EpCAM antigen.

To emphasize the role of the Ab2-GR-QDs bionanoconjugates in the signal amplification strategy; the immunosensor was fabricated under the similar experimental procedure, but with the exposure to Ab2-QDs instead of Ab2-GR-QDs bionanoconjugates (hereafter, referred to as ITO/PPA/Ab1/An/Ab2-QDs) (Fig. 4a(ii)). The Ab2-QDs conjugates were fabricated using EDC/NHS coupling reactions between the carboxylated-QDs and steptavidin/biotinylated antibodies (see Supplementary content for details). As can be seen in Fig. 4a (ii), the peak current of 5.2μA resulted for 10ng/mL EpCAM at the ITO/PPA/Ab1/An/Ab2-QDs immunosensor is about 6-times smaller than that of the ITO/PPA/Ab1/An/Ab2-GR-QDs immunosensor. The sensitivity enhancement at Ab2-GR-QDs bionanoconjugates based immunosensors was due to the large surface area of 2D-GRs, which bear a greater amount of QDs per immunological event compare to that provided by the Ab2-QDs conjugates.

Fig. 4b shows the dependence of the magnitude of oxidation peak currents on the concentration of EpCAM antigen in PBS. The oxidation current increased with increasing the EpCAM concentration from (a) 100fg/mL to (i) 7.5μg/mL (Fig. 4c). Each data point in the curve represents the average of the three responses obtained from three freshly prepared immunosensors. A similar current–concentration relationship has been reported previously in heterogeneous imuunosensor (Das et al., 2007; Hucknall et al., 2009). The detection limit (LOD) was estimated to be 100fg/mL. This is the lowest concentration for which the background-subtracted signal was greater than three standard deviations (3σ) above the average signal from the immunosensor that was exposed to PBS with no target EpCAM antigen. The LOD is about 2-orders of magnitude lower compared to those reported previously based on other amplification strategies employing QDs (Cui et al., 2007; Chen et al., 2009), carbon nanotube/enzymes (Yu et al., 2006), GR/carbon nanospheres/enzymes (Du et al., 2010), and GR/enzymes in buffer solutions (Du et al., 2011). Such a LOD also comparable to those obtained in the most sensitive chemiluminescent immunosensor based on the bio-barcoded nanoparticle tags (Lin et al., 2011) and GR/QDs composites (Li et al., 2011). This suggests that the proposed method is highly sensitive for the detection of tumor biomarker at low levels.

The selectivity of the proposed immunosensor for the detection of EpCAM antigen was tested against the PSA antigens. The BSA-treated ITO/PPA/Ab1 substrate was incubated in a mixture of EpCAM and PSA antigens in PBS for 1h followed by the incubation in PBS containing Ab2-GR-QDs at 37°C for 1h. The peak current obtained for a sample mixture solution containing 10ng/mL EpCAM and 10ng/mL PSA was similar to that obtained only for 10ng/mL EpCAM antigen (relative standard deviation (RSD)=6.3%, n =3; Fig. S4A). In another experiment, the ITO/PPA/Ab1 substrate was incubated in a mixture solution containing 100fg/mL EpCAM and 100fg/mL PSA antigens in PBS. As can be seen in Fig. S4B, the peak current for the sample with EpCAM and PSA antigens was essentially similar to that obtained only for EpCAM antigen (RSD of 8.2%, n =3). These support that the proposed immunosensor retain a good selectivity in detecting EpCAM against PSA in PBS buffer.

The reproducibility of the immunosensor performance was investigated by measuring the stripping peak currents with four freshly prepared sensors at 10ng/mL EpCAM antigen (Fig. S5A). A good reproducibility in stripping currents with the RSD of 7.1% (n =4) was obtained. The storage stability of the immunosensor was also investigated by comparing with the stripping currents after a sandwiched immunoreactions at 10ng/mL EpCAM antigen (Fig. S5B). About 78% of the original reaction activity retained after three weeks storage of the ITO/PPA/Ab1/An/Ab2-GR-QDs sensor in PBS at 4°C indicating a good storage stability for the immunosensor at 4°C.

To evaluate whether the proposed system could be used with biological fluids, the designated concentration of EpCAM (Fig. 4d) was spiked into human serum and detected via the proposed method. The control sensor (the ITO/PPA/Ab1 substrate was incubated in serum without the added EpCAM followed by the immunoreactions with Ab2-GR-QDs) displayed an significant peak current in serum (0.37μA) compare to that observed in buffer (0.25μA) (see Fig. 4d inset), reflecting either the presence of the EpCAM in serum or the nonspecific adsorption of bionanoconjugates on the sensing layer. The peak current observed for control sensor and the serum spiked with 100fg/mL and 500fg/mL of EpCAM are essentially similar. These results indicate that the immunoassay is not sensitive enough to detect 500fg/mL or less EpCAM antigen in serum, probably due to the nonspecific interaction of anti-EpCAM with serum proteins and/or nonspecific serum proteins adsorptions on the sensing layer. Therefore, the enhanced peak current for the control sensor might be responsible for the nonspecific adsorption of bionanoconjugates on the sensing layer. Clearly, the peak current measured for the serum spiked with 1pg/mL EpCAM was similar to that of the control plus three times the standard deviation of the background response. Based on this study, the detection limit was estimated to be 1pg/mL in serum. This value is 10-times higher than that obtained in the PBS buffer (e.g., 100fg/ml). Also the peak current obtained for serum spiked with 10pg/mL EpCAM was 25% less than that obtained for the similar concentration of EpCAM in PBS (4.11μA vs. 5.50μA). The reduction in peak currents and poor detection limit in serum are most likely due to the formation of the proteins complexes with EpCAM antigen and other biomolecules in serum or the nonspecific interaction of surface bound anti-EpCAM with serum proteins.

The high sensitivity of our method relies upon three types of amplification: (i) chemical conversion of ester, hydroxyl and epoxide groups of 2D-GR to carboxylic acid groups provides a high density of CdSe QDs and EpCAM antibodies. Because these bionanoconjugates contain several QDs for each EpCAM antibody which binds to a EpCAM antigen in a single sandwiching event, the binding of a single EpCAM antigen significantly amplified by dissolving the captured-QDs, and detecting the Cd2+ via SWASV; (ii) the ultrasensitive nature of the SWASV. It involves a preconcentration step (Bard and Faulkner, 2001), during which the dissolved metal ions are accumulated onto the detection electrode (Cd2+ +2e→Cd0) that gives rise to an enhanced peak during the ‘stripping’ process (Cd0 →Cd2+ +2e). Such a methodology represents a simple and accurate way to significantly/physically amplify the detection signal. We believe that the improvement in the sensitivity shown by the proposed method over previously reported graphene-based strategies (Tang et al., 2011; Du et al., 2011; Wan et al., 2011) will be beneficial for the detection of cancer biomarker at low level in serum-based samples.

We have successfully functionalized 2D-GRs with CdSe QDs and antibodies and demonstrated that these bionanoconjugates are highly sensitive as signal-transduction labels. The carboxylation of the 2D-GRs provides the enhanced active sites for the covalent attachment of the amine functionalized CdSe QDs, which in turn results in a higher sensitivity of the immunosensor. This method enables clinically important biomarkers in buffer and serum to be measured at 100fg/mL and 1pg/mL concentrations, respectively. The detection limit of 100fg/mL in buffer is much lower than the reported values found in many recent signal amplification strategies (Yu et al., 2006; Cui et al., 2007; Chen et al., 2009; Du et al., 2010, 2011). The method also exhibits a wide linear range (e.g., 6-order concentration range) and acceptable reproducibility (RSD 7.1%, n =4). In addition, the principle of the bionanoconjugate preparation and immunosensor fabrication described in this study is a generic one, where new immunosensors can be fabricated by changing the specific antibodies in the corresponding fabrication steps. Hence this method offers a new avenue for the detection of wide range of tumor biomarkers with enhanced sensitivity, and has significant potential for clinical sample analysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the UQ Postdoctoral Fellowship (RM 2010001241), UQ ECR Grant (RM 2010002245), and National Breast Cancer Foundation Collaborative Research Grant.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2012.02.057.
